title: Will AI make companies outsource more, or less?
summary_raw: Maybe both.

summary_ai.digest: Noah Smith argues AI will simultaneously drive outsourcing and insourcing depending on task specificity, labor economics, and coordination costs—collapsing the binary framing most forecasters use.
summary_ai.summary_long: Smith challenges the conventional wisdom that AI inevitably centralizes or decentralizes work. Instead, he suggests AI acts as a differential cost shifter: it makes some tasks cheaper to automate in-house (reducing outsourcing), while making other tasks easier to coordinate remotely or with specialized vendors (increasing outsourcing). The net effect depends on whether AI's efficiency gain exceeds the overhead of managing external relationships. This framework reframes debates about manufacturing reshoring, offshoring, and the future of contract labor as empirical questions about task-level economics rather than sector-wide predictions.
talking_points: AI doesn't have a direction on make-vs-buy; it changes the cost structure of coordination and automation unevenly across tasks.
Reshoring and further outsourcing can happen in the same company—some functions come in-house as AI makes them cheaper to automate, others go out as AI cuts the friction of remote collaboration.
This logic applies to banking workflows: some compliance and risk functions may centralize, while fintech-bank partnerships may deepen if AI lowers the cost of real-time data sharing.
summary_ai.highlight: AI flips outsourcing decisions function-by-function, not sector-wide—reshoring and deepening partnerships are not mutually exclusive.

title: Politically Incorrect Paper of the Day: The US Racial Wealth Gap
summary_raw:
Writing in the QJE, Derenoncourt, Kim, Kuhn, & Schularick argue that today’s black-white wealth gap can be explained by differences in initial conditions from over a hundred and fifty years ago, i.e. slavery. But there is an important, and glaring objection: in the age of immigration (1850–1924) millions of whites immigrated to the United States […]

The post

summary_ai.digest: Derenoncourt et al. (QJE) trace today's racial wealth gap to slavery-era initial conditions, but Cowen flags a counterargument: millions of white immigrants arrived post-1850 without inherited wealth yet converged to majority outcomes.
summary_ai.summary_long: The paper argues that persistent Black-white wealth gaps today are rooted in slavery and Reconstruction-era dispossession, using historical initial conditions as the primary explanatory variable. Cowen identifies a critical tension: if initial conditions fully explain gaps, how do we account for European immigrants who arrived with zero wealth accumulation history yet achieved parity with native whites? The implication is that either the initial-conditions model is incomplete, or intervening mechanisms (access to credit, labor markets, asset appreciation, policy) matter more than the model suggests. This framing opens a debate about path-dependency vs. contemporaneous barriers.
talking_points: If slavery-era wealth explains today's gap, why did post-1850 white immigrants without any inherited advantage reach parity with native whites?
The counterargument suggests the mechanism isn't just historical initial conditions but something about how different groups access credit, real estate, and wealth accumulation in the present.
This highlights a methodological tension: explaining persistence vs. explaining convergence within the same historical window.
summary_ai.highlight: Cowen's immigrant-parity observation directly challenges the sufficiency of initial-conditions explanation and reframes the debate toward contemporaneous access and policy.

title: ‘I claimed Social Security at 62’: At 76, I’m working at Walmart. Why do I still owe payroll taxes?

summary_ai.summary_long: Social Security beneficiaries who work continue to owe payroll taxes (FICA) regardless of age or prior claiming decisions. Once full retirement age is reached, the earnings test no longer applies—meaning no benefits are withheld—but employment-income taxation persists. The article touches on the broader trend of older workers remaining in the labor force, often out of financial necessity rather than choice. This raises implicit questions about adequacy of early-claim decisions and retirement savings shortfalls, but remains framed as a personal-finance puzzle rather than a systemic labor or Social Security policy issue.
talking_points: Payroll taxes don't stop when you reach full retirement age; you still owe FICA on W-2 wages, even if no benefits are withheld.
The earnings test (which reduced benefits for those claiming early) disappears at full retirement age, but that doesn't exempt you from employment taxes.
Anecdotal evidence of heavy 65+ representation in retail suggests either inadequate retirement savings or a structural shift in late-career labor supply.
summary_ai.highlight: Once full retirement age is reached, no benefits are withheld for earnings, but FICA obligations continue—a distinction many early claimers don't understand until they re-enter the workforce.

Dithering, Sharp Tech, and Sharp China will also return the week of July 6. Greatest of All Talk and Asianometry will continue to publish. The full Stratechery posting schedule is here.

title: I’m 53 and want to retire in 12 years. Is 5% enough to put in my 401(k)?
summary_raw: If you want to have enough for retirement, you need to push your savings limits today.

summary_ai.digest: Generic retirement-savings advice for retail investors with no FIG or macro market signal.
summary_ai.summary_long: A MarketWatch personal-finance column addresses a reader's retirement timeline and 401(k) contribution rate, offering the standard guidance that 5% savings is likely insufficient and that higher contributions are needed to meet retirement goals. The piece walks through basic financial-planning math (replacement ratios, catch-up contributions, compound growth) but contains no forward guidance on rates, credit conditions, consumer behavior trends, or implications for bank deposit flows, lending demand, or the financial services sector.
talking_points: Not applicable—this is consumer personal-finance advice, not a market or sector signal.

title: Going back to the office may actually be better for you than working from home
